VOLUME: 04, ISSUE: 10 I MAY 16 2026 ON THE COVER: DESIGNING VIPIN AGARWAL I PHOTO: MAHDI CHAGHARI

4-Editorial: Humanity at its most honest 23-Should horses still perform? 5-Will we all eat differently by 2050? 26-Is wine losing its charm?

11-The force that built civilization 29-Letters

world?
MAY 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 3

The Last Sunrise EDITORIAL Humanity Editor-in-Chief
MAGAZINE
Dr. Kamran Zuberi at its most honest Social & Lifestyle Editor
Dr. Uzma Naaz
hat would you do if tomorrow Art & Culture Editor never came? It sounds like one Dr. Monika Garg Wof those late-night questions people ask half-jokingly over coffee or dur- Digital Strategy Editor ing long train rides. Yet every few years, the Vishakha Sharma world seems to brush against that fear for real. A pandemic empties streets. A war flashes across television screens. Wildfires Social Media Editor turn entire skies orange. Suddenly, the im- Sushil Kumar possible no longer feels distant. And strangely, when people think the Art & Graphics Director end may be near, they don’t rush toward Vipin Agarwal ambition. They move toward each other. During the early months of COVID-19, there were stories from almost every country that felt deeply human. Neighbours left EDITORIAL ADVISORY BOARD groceries outside doors for elderly strangers. Families rediscovered dinner tables. Friends called each other without needing a reason. In cities usually obsessed with D. Bisht Shandilya (Former News speed, life slowed down just enough for people to ask themselves a difficult question: Editor, United News of India, New What actually matters? If the world ended tomorrow, most of us probably wouldn’t spend today checking Delhi), Prof. Amar Garg (Former unread emails or arguing online with people we’ll never meet. We’d call our parents. Vice Chancellor), Dr. Dharamveer We’d apologise to someone. We’d sit quietly with our children, or our partners, or Mahajan (Former Principal), Prof. maybe just ourselves. Some would pray. Some would write. Others would walk out- Archana Sharma (Pol. Science), side and look carefully at the sky, perhaps for the first time in months. Prof. P.D. Sharma (Botanist), Shahid Modern life rarely gives us room to think this way. We’re trained to plan endlessly A Siddiqui IRS (Retd.) for the future. Better jobs, bigger homes, longer savings plans. There’s nothing wrong with that, of course. Progress matters. Stability matters. But somewhere along the way, many people have started postponing joy itself. We save conversations for later. SUBSCRIPTIONS Vacations for later. Forgiveness for later. Living, oddly enough, also gets pushed to later. Maybe that’s why the idea of “the end” unsettles us so much. Not because life Phone +91 20 40199102 would stop, but because we realise how much of it we haven’t fully lived. Contact us Survey Number 12/1, There’s also another uncomfortable truth. If tomorrow were truly the last day, the Baner Road, world would not end equally for everyone. Some people would face it from safe Pune-411045 (Maharashtra) homes with loved ones around them. Others, even now, are already living through Email mail@magazine.revoir.in conditions that feel apocalyptic: war zones, refugee camps, hunger, displacement, Website www.revoir.in loneliness. For them, the question isn’t philosophical. It’s painfully real. And yet humanity continues in small acts of stubborn hope. A teacher still enters a Legal Advisor classroom. Doctors work exhausting shifts. Young people still fall in love, plant trees, Anil Bakhshi (Advocate) make music and dream about futures they may never completely control. Maybe that’s the point. If the world ended tomorrow, what would survive until the final moment would not be wealth or status. It would be kindness. Memory. Connec- tion. The simple proof that we were here and that, despite everything, we tried to care Copyright © 2026 REVOIR (eZine), for one another. Perhaps we shouldn’t wait for the end of the world to start living like all rights reserved. This publication that. may not be reproduced in any form without permission.
Khzuberil Dr. Kamran Zuberi Editor in Chief zuberi@magazine.revoir.in
4 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MAY 16 2026

IN-DEPTH
The future diet
Food has always been deeply emo- tional. A recipe can carry memories of Will we all eat grandparents, childhood summers or dif- ficult times survived together. People don’t just eat nutrients. They eat com- fort, identity and culture. That’s why conversations about changing diets differently world eats is becoming impossible to ig- quickly become personal. Still, the pressure to rethink how the
nore. By 2050, the global population is ex- pected to approach 10 billion. Climate by 2050? change is already affecting harvests, water supplies and food prices. Heat- waves damage crops. Droughts shrink farmland. Meanwhile, millions still struggle to afford healthy meals while By 2050, dinner tables could look very different from food waste continues on a massive scale. The question is no longer whether those of today. By Naina Kapoor diets will change. It’s how far they’ll change and whether people are truly ready for it. hat’s for dinner? sounds like beef. A teenager mixes cricket powder such an ordinary question. into a breakfast smoothie because it’s Why food systems are under strain WYet for scientists, farmers and cheap, high in protein and environmen- Modern agriculture feeds billions, families across the world, it’s becoming tally friendly. In another apartment, cul- but it comes at a cost. one of the biggest questions of the cen- tivated chicken grown from animal cells Livestock farming contributes signif- tury. heats inside a microwave. It sounds fu- icantly to greenhouse gas emissions and Picture a kitchen a few decades from turistic, maybe even slightly uncomfort- uses enormous amounts of land and now. Someone grills a plant-based steak able. But pieces of that future already water. Producing beef, for example, re- that looks and smells almost identical to exist. quires far more resources than growing
MAY 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 5

more regularly test meat alternatives because compli- demand continues to grow. cated. Part of the success comes from famil- Food iarity. Plant-based eating isn’t entirely disgust is new. Many traditional diets already rely power- heavily on vegetables, lentils and grains. ful. A In India, for example, generations have person grown up eating protein-rich vegetarian may un- meals without considering them un- derstand usual. the envi- What has changed is the technology. ronmen- Food companies can now create tal bene- plant-based products that imitate the tex- fits of ture, smell and appearance of meat sur- eating prisingly well. Some alternatives even insects “bleed” like beef. For younger urban beans or lentils. As cities expand and natural resources become more limited, governments and researchers are search- ing for alternatives that are both sustain- able and affordable. At the same time, younger genera- tions are already changing their eating habits. Many Gen Z consumers are more cli- mate-conscious than previous genera- tions. Some are reducing meat consump- tion for environmental reasons. Others are motivated by health concerns, ani- mal welfare or curiosity. Vegan milk, meatless burgers and high-protein plant snacks have moved from specialty stores into everyday supermarkets. But changing food habits is never simple. and still feel uncomfortable taking a People grow attached to familiar bite. That reaction isn’t always rational, meals. Traditions matter. In many cul- but it’s real. “ tures, food represents celebration, Companies trying to popularize in- People don’t family and even national identity. Ask- sect protein have noticed this barrier. In- ing people to replace those foods can stead of selling whole insects, many feel less like a health recommendation now use cricket flour in protein bars, just eat and more like a challenge to their way pasta or baked foods where consumers of life. barely notice the source. nutrients, they Even then, resistance remains. The rise of insect protein There’s also an uncomfortable social eat memory, For many people, the idea of eating question beneath the debate. Some insects still feels strange. critics worry that sustainable diets may comfort and Yet insects are already part of tradi- become divided by class. Will wealthy tional diets in many regions of the consumers continue enjoying expensive world. In countries such as Thailand and traditional meats while ordinary people identity.” Mexico, crickets and grasshoppers are are encouraged to eat insects in the commonly eaten as snacks or ing- name of sustainability? The concern redients. What seems unusual in one may sound cynical, but it reflects gen- consumers, these foods feel less experi- culture may be perfectly normal in uine anxiety about inequality. mental than they might have a decade another. ago. Scientists often point to insects as Plant-based eating goes Still, not everyone is convinced. one possible solution to future food mainstream Some nutrition experts argue that shortages. Crickets require far less water Compared to insects, plant-based heavily processed plant-based foods and land than cattle, while also produc- proteins have found acceptance much shouldn’t automatically be considered ing fewer emissions. They’re rich in faster. healthy. Others feel uncomfortable with protein, iron and vitamins. Restaurants now serve mushroom long ingredient lists and artificial flavor- On paper, the logic is convincing. burgers, soy kebabs and oat-milk coffees ing. Emotionally, however, the situation is almost everywhere. Fast-food chains And honestly, some products simply
6 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MAY 16 2026

and health data could become common. Vertical farms inside cities may grow vegetables year-round. Smart kitchens may help reduce food waste automati- cally. Yet even with all these changes, people will probably remain emotionally attached to comfort food. Someone somewhere will still crave homemade soup, fresh bread or street food prepared the old-fashioned way. Technology may transform ingredients, but the emotional role of meals will likely remain unchanged. That’s why conversations about fu- ture diets feel so emotional. They touch something deeply human. Nobody wants culture reduced to sta- tistics and carbon calculations. Yet ig- noring environmental realities also carries consequences.
Learning to eat differently The future of food may depend less on science alone and more on storytell- ing. People rarely change habits because of facts and charts. They change when new behaviors begin to feel normal. A child raised on plant-based school lunches may never see them as unusual. A teenager watching athletes promote alternative proteins may become curious don’t taste convincing yet. synthetic food production? Entire rural enough to try them. That matters more than people admit. communities depend on livestock farm- History shows how quickly food cul- Consumers may care about sustainabil- ing. Food transitions don’t only change tures can evolve. Tomatoes were once ity, but they also want food that feels diets. They change jobs, economies and feared in parts of Europe. Sushi was satisfying after a long day. identities. considered strange in many Western In some countries, debates around countries only a few decades ago. Meat without the farm cultivated meat have already become What feels unfamiliar today may Perhaps the most controversial idea political. Food carries symbolism. A eventually become ordinary. is cultivated meat, sometimes called lab- steak isn’t just protein. It can represent By 2050, dinner tables could look grown meat. tradition, masculinity, celebration or very different from those of today. There Unlike plant-based substitutes, culti- family history. may be more sustainable ingredients, vated meat is real animal meat produced Changing those emotional connec- fewer assumptions about where protein from animal cells rather than slaugh- tions may take generations. comes from and greater awareness about tered animals. Scientists grow the cells the environmental cost of food choices. in controlled environments until edible Will the future diet be the same But there will also be resistance, nos- tissue forms. everywhere? talgia and debate. Probably plenty of it. Supporters believe this technology Despite predictions of dramatic food After all, eating has never been just could dramatically reduce animal suffer- revolutions, the future diet may not be- about survival. It’s about memory, ritual ing and environmental damage while al- come completely uniform. and connection. lowing people to continue eating famil- Different regions will likely adapt in And perhaps the future diet won’t be iar foods. different ways. Wealthier cities may em- defined by one revolutionary invention But public reactions remain mixed. brace cultivated foods more quickly, at all. Maybe it will simply reflect a qui- For many consumers, laboratory-pro- while rural communities continue rely- eter shift in mindset, an understanding duced meat sounds unnatural. Even if ing on traditional farming. Climate con- that every meal connects people not scientists insist it’s safe, people often ditions will also shape eating habits. only to culture and family, but also to hesitate when food feels too engineered Areas facing severe drought may adopt the future of the planet itself. or unfamiliar. Trust becomes essential. resource-efficient foods out of necessity The next time someone asks, There are also economic concerns. rather than preference. “What’s for dinner?”, the answer may What happens to traditional farmers Technology will influence diets too. reveal far more about the world than we if large corporations begin dominating Personalized nutrition based on genetics realize. ■
MAY 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 7

SPECIAL REPORT
The digital trap
Life in a world being unreachable almost feels irrespon- sible. We wake up to alarms on our phones and fall asleep beside them. Work follows us home. Friends expect instant replies. News alerts interrupt dinner. Social media fills the tiny pauses that never tion, or simply looking out of a window. that once belonged to boredom, reflec- Technology promised connection and in many ways, it delivered. Families separated by oceans can now talk every day. Remote work has opened opportu- switches off nities for people who once had none. Students can learn almost anything on- line. Emergencies can be handled faster. The digital world has made life more ef- ficient, more informed and sometimes We were promised a world more connected than ever more compassionate. But somewhere along the way, con- before. So why do so many people feel mentally stant connectivity stopped feeling like exhausted and emotionally distant? By Riya Verma freedom. For many people, it started feeling like pressure. When the brain never truly rests ust one more notification. That’s friend’s meme, then opening a work chat Human beings were never designed what Maya told herself at 1:17 in again because the green dot beside her to process this much information all the Jthe morning while staring at the manager’s name made her anxious. By time. blue light of her phone. She had planned the time she finally put the phone down, A generation ago, most people had to sleep early after another draining her mind was racing. natural boundaries in their day. Work workday, but one unread message turned The strange thing is, Maya isn’t un- ended when they left the office. News into checking emails, then scrolling usual. Millions of people now live in a arrived in newspapers or on television at through headlines, then replying to a world where silence feels unfamiliar and fixed hours. If someone wasn’t home,
8 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MAY 16 2026

sages has become surprisingly challeng- ing for many. Some teachers now speak openly about students struggling to sustain at- tention in classrooms. Employers com- plain about declining focus while simul- taneously expecting workers to remain constantly available online. It’s a contra- diction that modern professionals know too well. You’re expected to be productive, creative, emotionally balanced and al- ways reachable. That’s exhausting. Burnout doesn’t always look dramatic
thing urgent might happen the moment Burnout is often imagined as a com- they stop checking their devices. And plete breakdown, but in reality, it can honestly, sometimes something does. look painfully ordinary. That’s part of the trap. It’s the employee answering emails you simply waited. from bed before sunrise. The freelancer Now there’s no real “off” switch. The attention economy and the who feels guilty taking a Sunday after- A marketing executive in Mumbai exhaustion behind it noon off. The young professional who checks campaign updates during dinner. Most digital platforms are designed keeps saying “I’m fine” while secretly A university student in London revises to keep people engaged for as long as feeling emotionally numb. for exams while responding to group possible. Notifications are carefully Remote work blurred the line be- chats every few minutes. A father in To- timed. Infinite scrolling removes natural tween professional and personal life ronto sits beside his child at the play- stopping points. Algorithms learn emo- even further. During the global shift to- ground while mentally drafting replies tional triggers with remarkable preci- ward working from home, many people to Slack notifications. sion. initially celebrated the flexibility. No The body may be resting, but the Attention has become a business commute. More comfort. More freedom. mind often isn’t. model. Yet over time, living rooms became Psychologists have increasingly There’s a reason people often pick up offices and office hours stretched end- linked excessive screen exposure and their phones without even realizing it. lessly. digital overload to anxiety, sleep disrup- The habit is deeply conditioned. Tiny For some workers, especially tion, burnout and reduced attention span. bursts of information, likes, messages younger employees trying to prove What makes it difficult is that the stress and updates create short moments of themselves, online visibility became a rarely arrives dramatically. It builds stimulation that the brain begins to form of performance. If they responded slowly, quietly. A person becomes more crave. But constant stimulation comes quickly, they appeared committed. If irritable. Sleep gets lighter. Concentra- with a hidden cost. they disconnected, they worried about tion weakens. Small breaks disappear. People are finding it harder to focus seeming lazy or unavailable. The pres- Even moments of relaxation start feeling deeply on one thing. Reading long ar- sure isn’t always spoken aloud, but it oddly restless. ticles feels difficult. Conversations are lingers. Many people describe an almost con- interrupted by glances at screens. Even In hyper-connected workplaces, stant sense of anticipation, as if some- watching a film without checking mes- people often carry the emotional weight
MAY 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 9

sing something important. Others worry that stepping back from online spaces could damage their careers or social lives. And to be fair, those fears aren’t completely irrational. Modern systems reward responsive- ness. Fast replies are praised. Visibility creates opportunities. Online networking shapes careers. Technology itself isn’t the villain here. Most people aren’t asking to aban- don modern life or throw away their de- vices. The issue is balance and balance becomes difficult when entire industries profit from keeping human attention permanently occupied. Relearning how to be unavailable There are small signs that people are beginning to push back. Some companies now encourage em- ployees to avoid after-hours communi- cation. Schools in certain countries are experimenting with phone-free class- rooms. Families create screen-free dinner rules. Young adults increasingly speak about protecting mental health in ways previous generations rarely did. Even tiny habits can matter. Turning off non-essential notifications. Taking walks without a phone. Reading without multitasking. Allowing boredom to exist again. These actions sound simple, maybe even trivial, but they restore something of being “on call” even when nobody Children are growing up observing important: uninterrupted human atten- explicitly asks them to. That invisible adults who rarely disconnect. Teachers tion. Because attention is more than pro- stress accumulates. and child psychologists increasingly ductivity. It shapes relationships, mem- worry about how this shapes emotional ory, creativity and emotional stability. Relationships are changing too development, patience, empathy and And perhaps that’s the real question Connectivity has transformed human communication habits. modern society must confront. If every relationships in complicated ways. A child trying to speak to a parent spare moment is occupied, when do On one hand, digital communication who keeps glancing at notifications no- people actually process their lives? helps people maintain contact across tices more than adults realize. So does a Late one evening, Maya finally continents and time zones. Long-dis- partner. began leaving her phone outside the tance friendships survive because of bedroom. At first, she hated it. She kept video calls and messaging apps. Parents The strange guilt of slowing down imagining missed messages and unfin- can see their children instantly. Support Perhaps one of the saddest con- ished conversations. But after a few networks exist online for people who sequences of constant connectivity is weeks, she noticed something unex- once felt isolated. that rest itself now feels uncomfortable pected. Her thoughts became quieter. Still, many people quietly admit they for many people. She slept longer. She started reading feel lonelier than ever. Moments that once allowed mental again. Some mornings, she simply sat A group of friends may sit together at recovery are quickly filled with scroll- near the window with coffee before a café while each person scrolls through ing. Waiting in a queue, sitting on a opening a screen. separate feeds. Couples share homes but train, even walking alone often becomes Nothing about her life became per- compete with screens for attention. Par- another opportunity to consume infor- fect. The world remained noisy. Work ents document childhood moments on- mation. People say they’re over- still demanded attention. Notifications line while occasionally missing the mo- whelmed, yet silence can feel strangely still waited. But she no longer felt ment itself. unsettling. owned by them. There’s a subtle emotional difference Some individuals who attempt digital Maybe that’s what people are really between being connected and being detoxes describe withdrawal-like symp- searching for now. Not complete discon- present. One offers access. The other toms during the first few days. They feel nection, but permission to breathe inside offers attention. anxious, disconnected, or afraid of mis- a world that rarely stops talking. ■
10 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MAY 16 2026

FEATURE STORY
fire. Historians often describe the con- Light trol of fire as one of humanity’s greatest turning points. It changed far more than cooking habits. Fire extended the day beyond sunset, allowing people to com- The force that municate, plan and tell stories after dark. Communities formed around its warmth. Imagine that moment thousands of years ago. Beyond the fire lay darkness, cold and danger. Inside the circle of or- built civilization safety. That glow did more than protect ange light, there was conversation and people. It connected them. Over time, civilizations learned to shape light in more sophisticated ways. Different moments. Same force. Perhaps that’s why Oil lamps illuminated ancient streets. Temples in Egypt were designed so sun- light remains one of humanity’s most enduring light entered sacred chambers only at particular times of the year. In India and symbols. By Karan Malhotra China, lamps became woven into rituals and festivals. Light slowly became a symbol of hope, wisdom and spirituality. Humans are visual creatures. We un- hat would happen if the It has always been emotional too. For derstand the world largely through what world suddenly went dark? It ancient humans, light meant safety. For we see. In many ways, light gave civ- Wsounds dramatic, almost cine- sailors, it meant direction. For artists, in- ilization not just survival, but meaning. matic, but the question reveals some- spiration. For scientists, discovery. Even thing important about human history. today, in cities glowing with billboards Light and the rise of knowledge Nearly every stage of civilization, from and phone screens, people still light can- For centuries, the study of light trans- the first cave fires to modern fiber optic dles during grief, prayer and celebration. formed science itself. One of the earliest networks, has been shaped by light. We Somehow, light continues to feel deeply breakthroughs came from wake with it, work beneath it, celebrate human. entity["people","Ibn al- around it and depend on it in ways we Haytham","Islamic scholar and pioneer rarely notice. The first flame of optics"], whose work on vision and Light has never been only scientific. Long before electricity, there was optics challenged long-held assumptions

about how humans see. His experiments doctors use lasers for delicate surgeries. But artificial light also changed helped establish principles that later in- Knowledge still travels through illumina- human behavior in complicated ways. fluenced modern scientific methods. tion. People became less connected to natural Then came telescopes. When en- rhythms. Today, millions of children tity["people","Galileo Galilei","Ital- The glow that changed cities growing up in large cities rarely see a ian astronomer and physicist"] ob- For most of history, night limited sky filled with stars because of light served the night sky through a telescope, human activity. Suddenly, ordinary mo- more clearly. developed through the work of many in- ments could survive time. Even today, observatories search for ventors, became a unique symbol of pro- A grandparent’s portrait. A child faint light from distant galaxies, while gress itself. running through rain. A soldier writing
12 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MAY 16 2026

“
The same light that once guided sailors
across oceans now carries billions of
digital conversations through glass fibers.”
home during war. Such memories en- dure because someone learned how to preserve light on paper. Cinema took the relationship even further. Moving images projected through beams of light became power- ful enough to shape public imagination, politics and culture across the world. Painters and architects also became fascinated with light. The Impression- ists studied how sunlight transformed landscapes through the day, while mod- ern architects learned that natural light affects mood and mental well-being. Even now, people instinctively seek bright rooms and open windows. Per- haps humans still carry an ancient mem- ory of gathering around fire. tricity, offering cleaner energy in a has shaped how humans live, communi- warming world. cate, worship, create and dream. And its The invisible light powering Yet technology has complicated hu- influence continues quietly every day. modern life manity’s relationship with light too. Tonight, somewhere, a student will Much of today’s civilization depends People now spend hours staring at study under a desk lamp. A surgeon on forms of light people never actually glowing screens late into the night. Ar- will rely on laser precision in an operat- see. Fiber optic cables carry enormous tificial brightness surrounds modern ing room. An astronomer will watch an- amounts of information through pulses life, but many individuals feel mentally cient starlight arrive through a tele- of light. Every video call, online pay- exhausted and disconnected from na- scope. A family facing a power cut will ment and streamed film travels this ture. light candles and sit together talking. way. Perhaps civilization has reached a Different moments. Same force. Per- It’s remarkable when you think stage where it must rediscover balance. haps that’s why light remains one of hu- about it. The same force that once illu- manity’s most enduring symbols. Not minated caves now powers global com- A civilization written in light because it solves every problem, but be- munication. Human history could almost be re- cause it keeps guiding people forward. Medicine also relies heavily on light. told through the ways people learned to Civilization is often explained through Infrared imaging helps detect disease. create, study and control light. From wars, empires and inventions. But Ultraviolet light sterilizes equipment. campfires to lighthouses, stained glass maybe its real story is simpler than that. Solar panels convert sunlight into elec- windows to smartphone screens, light A small flame pushing back the dark. ■
MAY 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 13

EXCLUSIVE
The Last Day
If the world ended
hat would you do if you knew you had only one day left? WIt’s the kind of question tomorrow… people usually avoid. Too uncomfort- able. Too dramatic. Yet it slips into the mind after a tragedy, a frightening head- line or the sudden loss of someone we The thought of the world ending tomorrow sounds love. For a moment, the future no longer feels guaranteed. And then another terrifying, until it forces us to ask what truly matters thought follows: if the world truly ended tomorrow, how would people spend today. By Aarav Sharma today? Not as nations or political groups. Simply as human beings. The answer dark humor and conspiracy theories. Airports would fill with people trying might reveal more about humanity than Memes would appear within minutes be- to go home. Not for business meetings history books ever could. cause humor is often how people handle or holidays. Home. fear. A son working abroad would call his The first reaction: disbelief But beneath the noise, something mother repeatedly. A student living over- Human beings are remarkably skilled quieter would begin. People would start seas would want to hear her father’s at denial. During disasters and crises, re- reaching for each other. voice again. Divorced couples might sit searchers have often noticed that many together for dinner because resentment people continue ordinary routines at Home would suddenly matter suddenly feels too small. first. They check their phones, make tea, again Across cultures, the details would answer emails. Routine feels safer than Modern life has trained people to live differ, but the instinct would remain fear. at a distance. Families are scattered ac- similar. In Italy, families might gather If the world had exactly twenty-four ross countries. Friends communicate around long meals. In Japan, some could hours left, disbelief would arrive before through hurried messages. Parents are seek silence in temples or gardens. In panic. distracted by work, children by screens. India, prayers would rise from homes, People would refresh news websites, But if time became painfully limited, mosques, churches, temples and gurud- hoping the warning was wrong. Social priorities would rearrange themselves waras. media would explode with arguments, quickly. And somewhere, millions of people
MAY 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 15

“
In humanity’s final
hours, people
would probably
search for each other long before
they searched for
answers.”
For years, doctors and caregivers working with terminally ill patients have noticed a pattern. Near the end of life, people rarely talk about status or posses- sions. They talk about relationships, un- finished conversations, forgiveness and simple moments that once seemed ordi- nary. Maybe that’s why end-of-the-world stories fascinate us so deeply. They are not really about destruction. They are mirrors. They expose what matters when dis- traction disappears. Religious com- munities would gather. Young people might organize concerts or candlelight vigils. In crowded cities, strangers could become unexpectedly kind. Something similar happened during would fade. Stock markets, office pol- the pandemic years. Even while fear itics, luxury brands and follower counts spread, there were stories of neighbors would suddenly seem fragile, even ab- helping each other, healthcare workers surd. comforting isolated patients and musi- Would anyone care about quarterly cians performing from balconies. targets if the sky itself was collapsing? Human beings are imperfect. Selfish Probably not. sometimes, cruel sometimes. But history A final day would force people into also shows that people often become the present tense. The vacation post- deeply compassionate when faced with poned for years. shared vulnerability. The apology never made. The guitar left untouched in the corner. The child The digital world would feel asking a parent to look up from a laptop. strangely empty Modern culture encourages endless There’s another uncomfortable truth preparation for the future. Work harder. about modern life. Many people spend Save more. Plan constantly. There’s more time documenting experiences logic in that, of course. But many people than fully living them. Meals are photo- become so busy building life that they graphed before they’re tasted. Vacations forget to experience it. become content. Even grief sometimes would quietly think the same thing: “I turns into public performance online. wish we had spent more time together.” Some people would seek meaning If humanity truly faced its final day, Not everyone would spend the last that habit might suddenly feel meaning- The things we chase would lose day the same way. Some would pray. less. meaning Others would celebrate loudly. Some No one would care about perfect cap- Money would still exist tomorrow would sit quietly near the sea or walk tions or carefully edited images. People morning, technically. But its power through familiar streets. would want reality instead. Real voices.
16 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MAY 16 2026

“
The end of the
world would not
create meaning, it
would simply expose what
mattered all along.”
from a balcony. Sharing tea with some- confuses productivity with purpose. one you love. INSIGHT
WELLNESS OVER APPEARANCE
The new beauty comfortable in one’s own body. Not per-
fect. Just real. And honestly, that change may be one of the most important cultural shifts of this generation. revolution From beauty routines to emotional
survival For many people, beauty routines once promised control. A new cream, People are realizing that wellness is not separate from diet, fitness trend, or makeup technique beauty. It shapes it. By Pooja Mehta offered hope that confidence could somehow be purchased or applied. Sometimes those routines were en- joyable and creative. There’s nothing in- hat if the healthiest person in being observed, rated and compared. herently wrong with makeup, skincare, the room isn’t the one with But something has shifted. Slowly at or wanting to look good. The problem Wperfect skin, a tiny waist, or a first, then all at once. began when appearance became tied too carefully filtered Instagram feed? Today, more people are asking differ- closely to self-worth. A few years ago, beauty conver- ent questions. Am I sleeping enough? College students now speak openly sations often began with appearance and Why am I anxious all the time? Why about “pretty pressure,” the constant ended there too. Better hair. Flatter does self-care feel like another competi- feeling that they must look camera- stomach. Sharper jawline. Fewer wrin- tion? Why do I look “fine” online but ready even during ordinary moments. kles. Social media amplified it all, turn- feel emotionally drained offline? Office workers describe editing photos ing ordinary faces into polished digital The modern beauty conversation is before posting them because unfiltered performances. Many people, especially no longer only about appearance. In- images feel risky. Teenagers compare young adults, quietly carried the ex- creasingly, it’s about wellness, balance, themselves to influencers whose images hausting feeling that they were always mental health, confidence and feeling are often altered beyond recognition.
18 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MAY 16 2026

“
The new beauty
ideal isn’t
perfection
anymore, it’s peace of mind.”
this moment. Wellness is not always dra- matic transformation. Sometimes it’s simply the end of constant self-criticism.
Men are entering the conversation too Beauty pressure has long affected women more visibly, but men are in- creasingly confronting similar struggles. Fitness culture, grooming standards and social media trends have created un- realistic expectations for male bodies as well. Lean muscles, flawless skin, lux- ury lifestyles, emotional control. The modern masculine image can be just as restrictive. Many young men now openly dis- cuss burnout from trying to maintain im- possible standards online. Some speak about steroid use in gym culture. Others admit they feel uncomfortable express- The emotional cost has been signifi- bracing softer, more realistic goals. Get- ing insecurity because vulnerability still cant. ting enough sleep. Drinking water. carries stigma. Studies across several countries have Walking without obsessing over calo- Interestingly, wellness culture has linked excessive social media compari- ries. Taking breaks from social media. opened space for broader emotional con- son with anxiety, body dissatisfaction, Going to therapy. Learning boundaries. versations among men too. Meditation low self-esteem and loneliness, espe- Saying no without guilt. apps, therapy platforms, support groups cially among young users. Mental health These habits rarely create viral and mental health podcasts are drawing professionals say many patients no content, but they improve lives in qui- audiences that would have seemed un- longer separate appearance from iden- eter ways. likely a decade ago. tity. If they don’t feel attractive, they as- Take the growing popularity of The idea that caring for emotional sume they are failing socially too. phrases like “body neutrality.” Unlike well-being is “weak” is gradually losing That’s where the newer wellness body positivity, which encourages lov- ground. movement enters the picture. ing one’s appearance, body neutrality And perhaps that’s part of the new Instead of asking, “How do I look?” focuses less on appearance altogether. beauty revolution as well. Not looking more people are beginning to ask, “How The body becomes something to care for invincible all the time. do I feel?” It sounds simple, but cul- rather than constantly evaluate. turally, it changes everything. For someone recovering from years Brands are adapting, though not of insecurity, that idea can feel surpris- always perfectly The rise of softer goals ingly liberating. The beauty and wellness industries Not long ago, wellness itself became A 24-year-old marketing executive in have noticed the shift. Advertisements commercialized. Social feeds filled with Mumbai described deleting beauty increasingly feature diverse skin tones, green juices, expensive yoga retreats filters from her phone after realizing she body types, ages and disabilities. Cam- and impossible morning routines begin- no longer recognized her own face in paigns now speak about authenticity, ning at 5 a.m. Even “healthy living” photographs. “I didn’t suddenly become confidence and self-acceptance. started to look like a performance. confident,” she admitted in an online Some of these changes are meaning- Now there’s growing resistance to discussion forum. “But I stopped pun- ful. Others feel carefully calculated. that pressure too. ishing myself every day.” Consumers today are quick to rec- Young people are increasingly em- That sentence captures the mood of ognize performative messaging. A com-
MAY 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 19

pany cannot realistically preach self- love while heavily editing every cam- paign image. Young audiences, espe- cially Gen Z consumers, tend to value transparency more than polished perfec- tion. Several brands have responded by re- ducing retouching, featuring real cus- tomer stories, or supporting mental health initiatives. Even fashion cam- paigns are becoming slightly less rigid about idealized beauty. Still, contradictions remain. The same platforms promoting well- ness often continue rewarding unattain- able aesthetics through algorithms and advertising. Influencer culture still thrives on aspiration. Cosmetic proce- dures are increasingly normalized among younger age groups. So the shift toward wellness is real, but it isn’t complete. Society is still ne- gotiating what healthy beauty standards actually look like.
Digital life and the exhaustion of being visible Part of the reason this conversation feels urgent is because modern life rarely allows people to disappear. Previous generations could leave school or work and largely escape social judgment for the day. Today, visibility follows people home through phones, group chats, stories, video calls and end- less scrolling. There’s pressure to look attractive during workouts, vacations, casual out- may not be external at all. It’s internal. now sit beside conversations about skin- ings and even moments of vulnerability. People are beginning to understand care and style. Ordinary life has become strangely per- that wellness is deeply connected to People are realizing that wellness is formative. beauty in ways older conversations often not separate from beauty. It shapes it. Psychologists often describe this as ignored. Chronic stress shows up physi- “continuous self-presentation.” People cally. Loneliness affects sleep. Anxiety A quieter kind of confidence are not just living experiences anymore. changes posture, energy, concentration Not every revolution arrives loudly. They are also documenting, editing and and confidence. Some begin in ordinary moments. A presenting them. Meanwhile, emotionally healthy teenager posting an unfiltered photo- That can become exhausting. people often radiate qualities no cos- graph for the first time. A mother choos- In response, many users are inten- metic product can manufacture fully: ing rest over relentless productivity. A tionally pulling back. Some keep private warmth, calmness, humor, kindness, young man finally admitting he’s strug- accounts with close friends only. Others presence. gling emotionally. A friend deciding not post less frequently or take social media That doesn’t mean appearance no to apologize for gaining weight. breaks altogether. A growing number of longer matters. Humans will probably These moments rarely make head- people are rediscovering offline hobbies always care about aesthetics to some de- lines, but together they reveal something that have nothing to do with appearance gree. Fashion, grooming and self-ex- important. Many people are tired of per- at all. pression can be joyful parts of identity. forming perfection. Reading. Gardening. Painting. Run- But increasingly, beauty is being Maybe the future of beauty is not ning slowly without tracking every sta- viewed less as perfection and more as about becoming someone else at all. tistic. alignment. Looking like yourself. Feel- Maybe it’s about feeling whole enough Tiny rebellions, maybe. But mean- ing emotionally stable. Having energy. to stop hiding. ingful ones. Feeling safe in your own skin. That idea feels less glamorous than Perhaps that explains why conver- old beauty myths. But it also feels more Redefining what beauty means sations about therapy, burnout, bound- human. And perhaps that’s why it’s last- The most powerful aspect of this shift aries, nutrition, sleep and mindfulness ing. ■

In the 1960s, Western fashion maga- The most women from beyond Europe. Gayatri zines became fascinated with royal Devi arrived at precisely the right mo- ment for global media hungry for exotic sophistication. Her photographs ap- beautiful woman and America, often accompanied by lan- peared in publications across Europe guage that now feels revealing in itself. She was presented as mysterious yet modern, regal yet approachable. The in the world? shaped her into an idea. world admired her beauty, but it also That idea reflected the beauty stan- dards of the time. Slim figure. Fair skin. Delicate features. Controlled poise. Perhaps her greatest charm was that she never Even today, many of the surviving mag- azine covers show how beauty was seemed to be performing for attention. ANALYSIS
Entertainment vs ethics
parades. In some places, they are sym- bols of prestige. In others, they’re tied to cultural identity and livelihoods. Yet ani- mal welfare advocates increasingly ar- Should horses gue that many of these practices survive not because horses need them, but be- cause humans are reluctant to let go. still perform? tivism versus tradition. It has become The debate is no longer emotional ac- more complicated than that.
A partnership shaped by history Humans and horses have shared a re- The larger issue may not be about horses alone. It’s lationship for thousands of years. Long
about how modern societies define acceptable before engines and highways, horses carried people across continents, worked entertainment. By Rohan Kapoor farms, delivered supplies and fought wars. Entire civilizations depended on them. That history matters because it o they enjoy this? It’s a simple ist photographs, another conversation shapes how many people still view question, the kind a child might has quietly grown louder. Not about horses today. MAY 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 23

“
A horse may obey
commands while
still experiencing
fear.”
more personal than sitting in a taxi. In cities like New York, Vienna or Marra- kech, horse-drawn carriages have long been part of the urban image itself. And then there’s the equestrian world. Competitive riding communities often argue that trained horses enjoy structured activity, bonding and stimula- tion. Many riders genuinely love and care for their animals. Some spend more on veterinary care than they do on them- selves. This is what makes the debate diffi- cult. Not every horse involved in enter- tainment is abused. Not every owner is careless. Still, affection alone doesn’t erase ethical questions.
When tradition becomes strain On a humid evening in South Asia, wedding bands play loudly while fire- works explode nearby. The groom sits lapsing on a Manhattan street from ap- dience. If a horse followed instructions upright on a decorated horse that inches parent exhaustion. Images spread online, calmly, people assumed it was comfort- nervously through traffic. That distinction changes the conver- than previous generations did. Young A horse named Ryder became a sym- sation. For decades, humans often audiences are more likely to see animals bol of this debate in New York after col- judged animal welfare by visible obe- as sentient companions rather than tools
24 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MAY 16 2026

many spectacles that later generations questioned. Public animal fights, captive dolphin shows and certain zoo practices once attracted huge crowds. Over time, ethical awareness shifted. That shift often begins with discom- fort. A small moment. A horse flinching at fireworks. A tired animal breathing heavily in traffic. A child asking a diffi- cult question. And honestly, people are conflicted because they still love horses. Perhaps that’s exactly why the debate feels per- sonal. There’s something undeniably mov- for spectacle. For them, a horse dancing ding horse owner may take pride in ing about these animals. Their size, sen- beneath bright lights may not appear keeping animals groomed and healthy. sitivity and quiet intelligence create magical. It may look uneasy. The problem is that good intentions emotional connection almost instantly. don’t always guarantee humane con- Watching a horse run freely across open The people behind the reins ditions. land carries a very different feeling from Yet removing horses from entertain- That’s why many welfare advocates watching one perform for noise and ment industries is not a simple moral now focus less on outrage and more on profit. equation. standards. They argue for mandatory Maybe the future lies somewhere be- Many families depend financially on rest periods, veterinary inspections, tween abolition and blind tradition. Not these traditions. MAY 16 2026 | REVOIR Magazine www.revoir.in 25

PERSPECTIVE Sobriety movement cape, is increasingly being examined through the lens of wellness. People are asking practical questions. How does drinking affect productivity? Mood? Re- lationships? Even skin health? And honestly, some don’t like the an- swers they’re finding. A growing number are embracing what’s often called “mindful drinking” or the “sober curious” lifestyle. It Is wine losing doesn’t always mean complete absti- nence. Often, it simply means drinking less, choosing carefully, or deciding al- its charm? piece of social life. cohol no longer needs to be the centre- That shift has opened the door for an industry many once dismissed as impos- sible: non-alcoholic wine.
For a growing generation, saying no to alcohol is no Wine without the buzz Not long ago, alcohol-free wine car- longer an act of rebellion, but an act of self- ried a bad reputation. Enthusiasts com-
awareness. By Anaya Gupta plained it tasted flat, overly sweet or strangely artificial. Even restaurants treated it like an afterthought.
ould you like the wine list, or our zero-proof pairings Wmenu? A few years ago, that question might have sounded odd in an upscale restaurant. Today, it feels sur- prisingly normal. In cities from London to Mumbai, New York to Melbourne, a quiet cultural shift is unfolding at dinner tables, rooftop parties and wedding re- ceptions. People are still gathering, laughing and celebrating. They’re just drinking differently. Wine, long associated with sophisti- cation, romance and social ritual, seems to be facing an uncomfortable question: does it still hold the same emotional grip on younger generations? The rise of mindful drinking For decades, wine carried a certain aura. A glass of red at the end of a stressful day symbolized maturity. Shar- ing a bottle over dinner meant intimacy, conversation, slowing down. Entire travel industries were built around vine- yards and tasting tours. It wasn’t just al- cohol. It was lifestyle. But attitudes are changing, especially among millennials and Gen Z con- sumers. Many young professionals now speak openly about anxiety, sleep quality, mental clarity and emotional balance in ways previous generations rarely did. Alcohol, once marketed as a form of es-
26 www.revoir.in REVOIR Magazine | MAY 16 2026

tional weight. In countries like Italy, France and Argentina, wine remains tied to family traditions, regional identity and everyday meals. That kind of her- itage doesn’t vanish overnight. And many wine lovers argue, fairly, that moderation was always part of wine culture at its best. The idea wasn’t ex- cess, but appreciation. Taste. Conver- sation. Slowness. Some also believe the sobriety move- ment risks becoming performative in its own way, another lifestyle identity shaped by wellness marketing and social media aesthetics. There’s probably some truth there too. Trends have a habit of turning personal choices into public branding. Still, something deeper seems to be Now, producers are investing heavily Younger adults are navigating burn- happening beneath the hashtags and cu- in improving quality. Vineyards in out, expensive cities and constant digital rated wellness routines. France, Spain, Australia and California overstimulation. Many don’t want hang- People are rethinking what comfort are experimenting with sophisticated de- overs consuming the little free time they means. They’re searching for connection alcoholisation techniques that preserve have. Others grew up watching older without dependency, celebration without aroma and texture. Some bottles even generations struggle quietly with alcohol regret, relaxation without escape. That mimic the complexity of traditional dependency and are consciously choos- emotional recalibration is bigger than wines surprisingly well. ing another path. wine alone. The market is responding. There’s also the visibility factor. In many urban bars, zero-proof Every dinner, party and late-night mis- The future of the social glass menus now sit proudly beside cocktail take can potentially end up online. That Maybe wine isn’t losing its charm so lists. Boutique stores dedicate shelves to awareness shapes behaviour more than much as losing its unquestioned status. alcohol-free sparkling rosé and botanical people admit. For centuries, alcohol occupied a wine alternatives. Social media in- Interestingly, the hospitality industry near-permanent seat at human gath- fluencers casually post photos of elegant isn’t resisting entirely. Many restaurants erings. Today, that seat is being shared. stemware filled with non-alcoholic and luxury hotels are adapting quickly Kombucha, botanical spirits, alcohol- blends and nobody seems particularly because they understand something im- free wines and simple sparkling water defensive about it anymore. portant: people still crave experience are entering spaces once dominated by What’s changed isn’t only the pro- and ritual. They still want beautiful Cabernet and Champagne. duct. It’s the social meaning attached to glasses, thoughtful pairings and cele- And perhaps that’s not a cultural loss. sobriety. bratory moments. They just may not Perhaps it’s just a broader definition of There was a time when refusing wine want intoxication attached to them every belonging. at a gathering invited awkward ques- time. A friend raising a glass of non-alco- tions. “Are you okay?” “Driving?” “On So the wine culture itself is evolving holic rosé at dinner is still participating medication?” Now, declining alcohol rather than disappearing. in the ritual. The conversation still can signal discipline, self-awareness or flows. The laughter still arrives in simply preference. That’s a major cul- Is wine really losing relevance? waves. The evening still matters. tural transformation. Not exactly. In the end, maybe the real story isn’t Global wine consumption has soft- whether people are drinking wine less. A generational reset ened in several regions, particularly It’s whether society is finally learning Part of this movement comes from among younger consumers, but wine that connection was never inside the economics and lifestyle realities too. still carries enormous cultural and emo- bottle to begin with. ■
